speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 2. It's July 20, 1995. We are in Sunrise, Florida. And we're interviewing Samuel Klasner, K-L-A-S-N-E-R. Mr. Klasner, you were telling me about leaving [? Limbrich ?] and going back home. What did you find when you got back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find very bad because we-- we couldn't make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother still in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother was in [INAUDIBLE], the oldest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Wolf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Adolf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adolf was in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was Bernard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernard was in Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he get to Brazil?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went for a visit before the war started. His sister was there, so he said he wanted to see what his sister is doing. Maybe he'll go and stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lola. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he has a visa to go to Brazil. He left to Brazil, but he wants to come back because he left his wife with a child. Then the war started, and they don't let him out. So that's the way he survived so many years more in Brazil. With your sister Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how they survived the war. And what about Rachel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel waw with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was still at home safe with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the conditions in Wolbrom had deteriorated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was food if you have money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was anybody able to still make money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jews treated any differently at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because they make the separation-- the Jews separate and the Gentiles separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they separate everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to move out. You have to live in the-- the buildings. You move out and stay together with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the Jews move to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they moved to district where the German tell them that over here they can stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to all those houses and residences where the Jews lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left the house too. I don't get nothing. The Pole-- Pole took me away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do with those places?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived. They moved in. They lived like kings there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any proclamations made against the Jews in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special. They were anti-Semitic, but not so that they show off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you made to wear Jewish stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have to have it. If not, you can't go. They're strict. If a German catch you, if you don't put on the star, you're finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have identity papers that identified you as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the people know that you were Jewish? How did the Germans know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Pole tell them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They tell them this is a Jew, this is a Jew. For a pound of sugar they sell to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sold the Jews for a pound of sugar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to all the Jewish businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of Jews they were hiding in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were hiding. But still they-- was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were allowed to go out on the streets at all times or were there restrictions?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 10 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would happen after 10 o'clock at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to stay home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if you went out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They catch you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would--

speaker: interviewee
text: If they catch you, you bad luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does bad luck mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: They kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any beatings of Jews on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes the Jewish people go to work or something like this for them. Roads to make it, you know, highways, different things you know. They have a German watching the groups. And when you stand for a second you talk to somebody, they hit you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you on any of these details? On any of these work details where you went out to build roads or do any of the work labor that the Germans asked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this what you did after you came back from [? Lembrook? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We make the roads.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did these roads go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from one city to the other city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And specifically, what did you do? Did you grade the road? Did you shovel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to shovel. We used to put cement.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was forced labor by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was labor for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not paid for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were barely fed food for this? And how old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was in 19-- end of-- no, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did life continue like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the finish. If they sent [INAUDIBLE], Juden-- Juden have it a sign. And they called you [GERMAN] Juden, [GERMAN] Juden. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they-- they hold [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the boys-- what they took it, they took it out all the young fellas-

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the war. And watchers was-- they were watching. A lot of things was from Russian or Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukraine's were watching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Watching the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they used the Ukraines as the guards over the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Used to hit them, used to do what they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could basically do anything to a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jew was very expendable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live this life where you were building roads for the Germans and the Ukrainians, and the Ukrainians were your guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they send me to Rzeszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Rzeszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in Poland. They send me there. Over there was a-- was a ghetto. But they don't send you to the ghetto. They send you to an airplane factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You was working in the airplane factory with the Pole. Over there, about 10,000 people were working.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point you were separated from your mother and your sisters and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They sent them to Trablinka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And us they sent to Rzeszow. But not all of them. They picked people what [? can ?] they see they can work and go over there. So in this-- in this factory, outside, they make buildings, barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Over there we was live. We went to the factory in the morning. We worked till at night. And then we went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: To barracks. And they have ready soup with a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's all you ate all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea at this point what had happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They said some of them-- the Pole said they took them-- they took with the train in the concentration.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew some people from your town were deported to concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where the concentration camp was at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they said that they take you in the-- the crematorium. They have it. And they give you a towel with a piece of soap and tell you to go get in the shower, but you don't come out. You went in, but there doesn't came out nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in this factory, what was your position, work position?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working on the lathe machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you manufacturing--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working lathe machine. We build motors to airplanes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was for German airplanes?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you-- do you remember the name of this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called them [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like for you? Were you treated decently by the people around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a little difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a day did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was two shifts-- night shift and day shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work both shifts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- both-- one week you was on the day time, and one week you went to the night time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they ran this factory 24 hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your day 10 hours long? How many hours a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10-12 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was the barracks to the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given clothes, proper clothes to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They give you just what you have it. And in other places they took away the private clothes. They don't let you own because they know you're going to run away or something like this. They cannot catch you.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this particular factory, did you have your own private clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was what you left home with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it adequate for the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what you have. You couldn't-- you couldn't have a choice in getting what you wanted, or when it was cold in winter time, that's what you have. You have to [INAUDIBLE] be happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you suffer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not too much. But still it was a struggle.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was already about 22 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about life?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't have a life. [LAUGHS] We just hope [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that will come a time when we're going to be free. But we didn't expect it to come out and be free and see what's going on in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you feel pessimistic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody-- nobody hoped. Nobody will believe it that we can survive what they were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you after this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this factory they, send us to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That is what I show you the picture from them-- Goethe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- this is his--

speaker: interviewer
text: His camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: --his baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Goethe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Goethe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Goethe was from-- he came from Austria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was a German SS, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was-- he looked after this particular camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was the father from the camp. But it was not so bad, because a lot of people went out to work, not inside in the camp, but outside in Krakow they got factories like Schindler. He has over 1,000 workers there. And he took out every day to go to the factory and then come back in this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you talk about Goethe, what sort of a man was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a murderer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was killing people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. He went around with two guns in the hand like this. And he see you, you walk. He don't like you when you walk. He shot you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then when they come from our side, not from the concentration, not from-- when people they catch them on the Gentile's paper they brought them in over there. And there was a hill they called-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I forget--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a hill outside of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was around digging a hole. And the people where they brought in, they don't-- they shoot them right away. Always-- he's all right, or he's healthy, or something like that-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they just exterminated?

speaker: interviewee
text: One on the other one was laid down and they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Goethe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ordered by Goethe. And he arbitrarily shot people whether he liked them or whether he did not like them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't matter. What were the conditions inside Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you repeat it?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions inside that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was barracks. People slept there. And they had factories like clothing factory, [? manufacturing, ?] and people were working sheet metal. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they watch. There was another guy, a German. He was really-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he was not a pure German. He came from Poland and German border. And there was a factory for clothing. They make the clothing for the armies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He hide himself. And when he came in, he jumped in to the window. And when he catch you, you slip, he kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] more over 20,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it men as well as women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but the camp was not separately. There was barracks for women and barracks for men. When they catch you in the same things, [LAUGHS] forget about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They kill you. Take you out and kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any people that you got close to while in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had a girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Let's see. Her name-- Irka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the same age as me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how were you able to meet with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when we meet. We could-- we could go in the girl barracks too. But not staying in the barracks overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during the day, they looked the other way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You could go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear of any news from the outside world while you were in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had a few Pole where they were working. And they brought the news.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they said it's not going to take too long, and we're going to be free. Took-- it took more than [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this camp I was until 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there for almost four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: About two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years. What physical condition were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not bad. I don't went to the doctors. I was healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I work. Did you have any word about your mother or your sisters or brothers the two years you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was worried, but what good is it to worry when you know you couldn't find any-- you couldn't know where they are? Or if they're still alive, or they're not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had no word, no idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you after this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this camp, they sent me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: They sent you to Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see, the Russian came near, and they were afraid that they going to catch them, and they're going kill them. So they took it-- Oskar Schindler took out 1,000 men and 200 women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp-- Plaszow. And they took it-- he wants to take us to Brunnlitz, where I show you the thing. Over there they have factories, textile factories, where they rebuilt the factory for ammunition. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we have to go to Gross Rosen [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Plaszow. In Gross Rosen we was about a couple weeks. And Oskar Schindler took us out to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you were selected for the Oskar Schindler list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he noticed I was a a metal-- sheet metal worker and toolmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Total--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he needs this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of plant did he have? What type of factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got to Gross Rosen? Did you know yet you were going to some munitions factory for Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said it when we are going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand who Oskar Schindler was at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, Oskar Schindler-- till 1943, he was an SS man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But in 1943 he changed his idea. And he knows that as the Russians come near [LAUGHS] he is not going to survive. So he took the people and brought them to Brunnlitz. And we rebuild the factory for ammunition. And he make the ammunition. The ammunition doesn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he purposely make the ammunition--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not working.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your conditions like while you were under the auspices of Oskar Schindler? How was your living conditions while you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have it just the same like in different [INAUDIBLE] barracks in camp. But one thing he was doing, if you don't have food-- and his wife too-- they went, and they trade cigarettes with alcohol for food. She make it that she has a birthday, and she give everybody a half a bread. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was looking if we need some medication or something else like this. She was doing very much a lot. I-- when I saw her here, I call her-- I ask her if I can call her 'second mother.' It's marked on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she remember you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She remembered me when she-- she saw my blue eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she saw your blue eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: When she was younger, she was a beauty. She was a beauty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel protected while you were under the care of Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know at that time that this was a very privileged place to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Beside this, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he has a SS man what was the top from-- from the-- from the-- from this-- this camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He wants to kill us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He wants to kill us. He make in the wood a grave for 1,200 people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was already a few-- very short with the Russian when they come. So he give us ammunition, guns. In case he cannot stop him to kill us, we should fight for our life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In time, he talk him in to throw hand grenades. And he throw to the next building, the next factory. And there started to be a fire. In not long, a couple of hours, two or three hours, they came from Berlin, and they took him away, because we was free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally meet with Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- what did you think of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I was knit sweaters. I learned, and I know that. I make for myself a sweater.

speaker: interviewer
text: You used to knit sweaters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Knit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to work at night, and the machine is automatic. [PAUSES FOR 60 SECONDS]
